title: User guide for Stats NZ’s employment measures | Stats NZ
content:
## User guide for Stats NZ’s employment measures
26 March 2021, 10:45am
User guide for Stats NZ’s employment measures is a valuable tool for policy analysts, researchers, journalists, and anyone else who is interested in statistics about employment in New Zealand.
Download the document or read it online.
- User guide for Stats NZ's employment measures Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 182 KB

### User guide for Stats NZ's employment measures
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 182 KB
The labour market framework is a function of supply and demand. The supply side relates to non-institutionalised persons aged 15 years and over who usually live in New Zealand and how they supply their labour. Households are shown to produce labour supply, in the form of jobless people (unemployed and not in the labour force) and employed persons. Paid employees, self-employed (subdivided into employers and self-employed without employees), and unpaid family workers make up employed persons. The demand side corresponds to labour demanded by businesses. It is represented by filled jobs and job vacancies (or unmet labour demand).
The count of employed persons and filled jobs have some overlap, in the form of paid employees filling a job. The difference between the count of filled jobs and paid employees can be attributed to factors like additional jobs held by multiple job holders and jobs filled by employees not within the usual resident population. Total employed persons also include some people who aren’t included in the filled job count, such as the self-employed and unpaid family workers.
Figure 1 The labour market framework
Text alternative for Filled jobs vs employed persons .
This guide provides an overview of Stats NZ data sources used to measure employment. The following tables show the key features and list the dimensions in published annual and subannual outputs. They are followed by summaries containing more detail about each source.
For more information, contact our Info Centre at info@stats.govt.nz or call 0508 525 525 (toll-free). Stats NZ is also available via live chat on Stats NZ’s website or Facebook page.

## Data sources for measuring employed persons
The Household labour force survey (HLFS) is the data source for official statistics about New Zealand’s labour force. As a survey of households, it contains relatively few business-level dimensions. It should instead be looked at for published household and individual-level dimensions less likely to be included in business employment measures, such as ethnicity or subjective opinions about work and labour opportunities. Additional information about employed persons is collected by a supplementary questionnaire in one quarter of the year. This is listed under the description of the HLFS.
Data sources for measuring employed persons
Data sources for measuring employed persons

The HLFS uses a short reference period of one week. The LEED annual data uses a one-year reference period. It covers everyone aged 15 years or over at the start of the tax year and wages or salaries, or self-employment income, reported in corresponding tax data. In the HLFS, a person is employed if they worked for one hour or more for pay or profit, or worked without pay for one hour or more in work that contributed directly to the operation of a business, or has a job but is not at work due to illness or is on leave. The published measure is an average calculated from totals in each week of the quarter. LEED annual data defines an employed person as someone who received wages or salaries, or income from self-employment, that is reported in tax data. Employment status is based on the source with the highest annual income if a person has multiple income sources during the tax year. The HLFS only collects earnings data in the June quarter of each year. The HLFS time series starts in March 1986 for total employed persons. The time series for employed persons by industry starts in September 2003. Source: Stats NZ
- The HLFS uses a short reference period of one week.
- The LEED annual data uses a one-year reference period. It covers everyone aged 15 years or over at the start of the tax year and wages or salaries, or self-employment income, reported in corresponding tax data.
- In the HLFS, a person is employed if they worked for one hour or more for pay or profit, or worked without pay for one hour or more in work that contributed directly to the operation of a business, or has a job but is not at work due to illness or is on leave. The published measure is an average calculated from totals in each week of the quarter.
- LEED annual data defines an employed person as someone who received wages or salaries, or income from self-employment, that is reported in tax data. Employment status is based on the source with the highest annual income if a person has multiple income sources during the tax year.
- The HLFS only collects earnings data in the June quarter of each year.
- The HLFS time series starts in March 1986 for total employed persons. The time series for employed persons by industry starts in September 2003.
Key dimensions in published outputs of employed persons
Key dimensions in published outputs of employed persons
(a) Workplace attributes
(a) Workplace attributes
(b) Person attributes
(b) Person attributes
Full-time/part-time status
Full-time/part-time status
Highest qualification
Highest qualification
Multiple job holding
Multiple job holding
(c) Employee attributes
(c) Employee attributes
Employment relationship
Employment relationship
Type of employment agreement
Type of employment agreement
Industry in LEED annual outputs is based on workplace locations for wage and salary earners. It is based on the location with the highest annual earnings if the person has worked for multiple employers during the tax year. Industry for self-employed persons is based on the enterprise owned or operated by them. In the HLFS, employment status distinguishes paid employees, employers, self-employed with employees, and unpaid family workers. Employment status in LEED annual outputs comprises wage or salary earners, and self-employed. It’s based on the highest annual income source if the person had multiple incomes sources during the tax year. Unpaid family workers and workers with earnings not reported in tax data are excluded. Region in LEED annual outputs is based on the region where the employed person has spent the most time during the year if they have lived in multiple regions. Age of employed persons in LEED annual outputs is at the start of the tax year. Youth is defined by the HLFS as persons aged 15–24 years. The HLFS collects job tenure data for the main job. Job tenure is the length of time a person has been working for their employer or in business if they are self-employed. Job tenure in LEED is defined as a period of successive months in which a person received earnings from the same employer in each month. Published outputs are about wage or salary jobs that are continuing as at the last day of the tax year. LEED outputs about multiple job holding relate to wage or salary jobs. The HLFS defines underemployed as people who are employed part-time and have both the desire and availability to increase their number of work hours. Source: Stats NZ
- Industry in LEED annual outputs is based on workplace locations for wage and salary earners. It is based on the location with the highest annual earnings if the person has worked for multiple employers during the tax year. Industry for self-employed persons is based on the enterprise owned or operated by them.
- In the HLFS, employment status distinguishes paid employees, employers, self-employed with employees, and unpaid family workers.
- Employment status in LEED annual outputs comprises wage or salary earners, and self-employed. It’s based on the highest annual income source if the person had multiple incomes sources during the tax year. Unpaid family workers and workers with earnings not reported in tax data are excluded.
- Region in LEED annual outputs is based on the region where the employed person has spent the most time during the year if they have lived in multiple regions.
- Age of employed persons in LEED annual outputs is at the start of the tax year.
- Youth is defined by the HLFS as persons aged 15–24 years.
- The HLFS collects job tenure data for the main job. Job tenure is the length of time a person has been working for their employer or in business if they are self-employed.
- Job tenure in LEED is defined as a period of successive months in which a person received earnings from the same employer in each month. Published outputs are about wage or salary jobs that are continuing as at the last day of the tax year.
- LEED outputs about multiple job holding relate to wage or salary jobs.
- The HLFS defines underemployed as people who are employed part-time and have both the desire and availability to increase their number of work hours.

## Data sources for measuring business employment
Business employment statistics measure the overlap of labour supply and labour demand or the point at which jobs are filled. Stats NZ uses six data sources. There is no single definitive data source because each has a different purpose. PAYE tax data is the basis of four data sources (MEI, BED, LEED, and BDS). All employers in New Zealand are required to report PAYE data, which includes earnings and tax deductions for each employee, to Inland Revenue (IR). The QES, MEI, and BED uses kind-of-activity units (or units with a single set of accounting records) as the statistical unit because they belong to the quarterly collection of business financial data. A common statistical unit and the same base data ensures coherence in economic statistics.

A short reference period is used by the MEI, QES, BED, and LEED to measure filled jobs. In contrast, the BDS uses a monthly reference period. Quarterly LEED data is based on monthly PAYE data. Its measures exclude employees aged under 15 years. In contrast, they are included by the other business employment data sources. A filled job is defined as a continuing employer-employee relationship. The MEI and BED use Monday to Sunday weeks in the calculation of average weekly filled jobs. FTEs in the QES is full-time filled jobs + 0.5 x part-time filled jobs Quarterly job flow measures in LEED include job creation and job destruction. The worker flow measures are accessions and separations. Employee count in the BDS is paid jobs, in the PAYE data, belonging to all wage and salary earners in February. Average weekly earnings are published by the QES for an FTE or filled job denominator. (Filled jobs is the sum of part-time and full-time filled jobs.) LEED releases mean and median quarterly earnings for full-quarter jobs, continuing jobs, and new hires. The MEI series from April 1999 to April 2019 is based on data from the Employer’s monthly schedule (EMS) PAYE tax form. From May 2019 onwards, the series is based on PAYE payday records. The BED series for quarters June 2011 to March 2019 is based on EMS data. From June 2019 onwards, the series is based on PAYE payday records. The LEED series for quarters June 1999 to March 2019 is based on EMS data. From June 2019 onwards, the series uses monthly PAYE data aggregated from payday records. The BDS series for years 2000 to 2019 is based on EMS data. From 2020 onwards, the series uses monthly PAYE data aggregated from payday records. Source: Stats NZ
- A short reference period is used by the MEI, QES, BED, and LEED to measure filled jobs. In contrast, the BDS uses a monthly reference period.
- Quarterly LEED data is based on monthly PAYE data. Its measures exclude employees aged under 15 years. In contrast, they are included by the other business employment data sources.
- A filled job is defined as a continuing employer-employee relationship.
- The MEI and BED use Monday to Sunday weeks in the calculation of average weekly filled jobs.
- FTEs in the QES is full-time filled jobs + 0.5 x part-time filled jobs
- Quarterly job flow measures in LEED include job creation and job destruction. The worker flow measures are accessions and separations.
- Employee count in the BDS is paid jobs, in the PAYE data, belonging to all wage and salary earners in February.
- Average weekly earnings are published by the QES for an FTE or filled job denominator. (Filled jobs is the sum of part-time and full-time filled jobs.)
- LEED releases mean and median quarterly earnings for full-quarter jobs, continuing jobs, and new hires.
- The MEI series from April 1999 to April 2019 is based on data from the Employer’s monthly schedule (EMS) PAYE tax form. From May 2019 onwards, the series is based on PAYE payday records.
- The BED series for quarters June 2011 to March 2019 is based on EMS data. From June 2019 onwards, the series is based on PAYE payday records.
- The LEED series for quarters June 1999 to March 2019 is based on EMS data. From June 2019 onwards, the series uses monthly PAYE data aggregated from payday records.
- The BDS series for years 2000 to 2019 is based on EMS data. From 2020 onwards, the series uses monthly PAYE data aggregated from payday records.
In addition to the measures in the above table, an experimental weekly employment indicator (WEI), based on PAYE payday records, is released by Stats NZ. It covers weekly paid job totals calculated from payday records available at 6, 20, and 34 days after the end of the week, and median weekly earnings.

Enterprise corresponds to a legal entity. A birth in Business demography is defined as a unit that existed at neither time T-1 year nor T-2 years. A death is a unit that existed at neither time T year nor time T+1 year. The BDS does not include KAU-level data. It only covers data belonging to enterprises and geographic units. KAU or kind-of-activity unit is a unit with a single set of accounting records. A KAU is the statistical unit used in quarterly business financial collections. A workplace location corresponds to a geographic unit. The QES can disaggregate filled jobs by workplace location, not gross weekly earnings and paid hours. The BED can disaggregate filled jobs by workplace location, not quarterly gross earnings. For more information and quotes for customised data, email info@stats.govt.nz or call 0508 525 525 (toll-free) Source: Stats NZ
- Enterprise corresponds to a legal entity.
- A birth in Business demography is defined as a unit that existed at neither time T-1 year nor T-2 years. A death is a unit that existed at neither time T year nor time T+1 year.
- The BDS does not include KAU-level data. It only covers data belonging to enterprises and geographic units.
- KAU or kind-of-activity unit is a unit with a single set of accounting records. A KAU is the statistical unit used in quarterly business financial collections.
- A workplace location corresponds to a geographic unit.
- The QES can disaggregate filled jobs by workplace location, not gross weekly earnings and paid hours.
- The BED can disaggregate filled jobs by workplace location, not quarterly gross earnings.
For more information and quotes for customised data, email info@stats.govt.nz or call 0508 525 525 (toll-free)

## Descriptions of data sources

### Household labour force survey (HLFS)
The HLFS provides a picture of New Zealand's labour force. It provides the key measures in the quarterly labour market release that combines data from the HLFS, QES, and LCI to present a broad picture of the labour market. For the latest data, see quarterly Labour market statistics .
New Zealand’s official quarterly labour force statistics
New Zealand’s official quarterly labour force statistics
Survey data collected from a sample of approximately 16,000 households in private dwellings
Survey data collected from a sample of approximately 16,000 households in private dwellings
Employed person coverage
Employed person coverage
Employed people aged 15 years or over who worked for pay or profit for one or more hours, including self-employed and unpaid relatives working in a family business
Employed people aged 15 years or over who worked for pay or profit for one or more hours, including self-employed and unpaid relatives working in a family business
Workplace definition
Workplace definition
Based on business or employer that person worked for
Based on business or employer that person worked for

Data used for producing estimates
All working-age persons in surveyed households
All working-age persons in surveyed households
Imputation for missing data?
Imputation for missing data?
Inaccurate respondent data and processing errors
Inaccurate respondent data and processing errors
Supplementary content can be added to a particular quarter of the HLFS to collect additional employment information. Two examples of supplements that are periodically added to the survey are Income statistics (collected by the HLFS in the June quarter of each year) and Working life (collected in the December 2018 quarter). Shorter supplementary topics such as Disability and volunteer work are added to the HLFS on a rotating or ad-hoc basis.
For more information about the HLFS supplementary programme, see Household surveys programme 2018–2022 .
The income supplement allows the analysis of links between labour force status, educational achievement, and the income of individuals and households.
For the latest data, see Labour market statistics (income) .
The Survey of working life collects data about job characteristics, the quality of work, employment conditions, and work arrangements of workers. Detailed information includes outputs about hours worked, work patterns and preferences, flexibility, security, working from home, and aspects of workplace health.
For the latest data, see Labour market statistics (working life) .
This supplement collects information about labour market participation and employment rates related to disabled workers as well as differences in wages and salaries received. The HLFS defines disabled people as those who have at least a lot of difficulty seeing or hearing, walking or climbing stairs, remembering or concentrating, taking care of themselves, or communicating.
For the latest data, see Labour market statistics (disability) .
This supplement is used to provide insights into the volume and type of voluntary work in New Zealand. It looks at the relationship between paid and unpaid work, and the characteristics of the volunteer workforce.
For the latest data, see Labour market statistics (volunteer) .

## Census of population and dwellings
The census takes a snapshot of people in New Zealand and is the official count of people and dwellings in New Zealand. Historically, the census has been held every five years, with some exceptions. The data is useful for counts of employed persons in areas or population groups that are too small for HLFS estimates. For the latest data, see 2018 Census .

## Annual Linked employer-employee data (LEED)
This is a comprehensive database made up of quarterly LEED, augmented with self-employment data from annual tax returns. It’s designed to produce person-level outputs about taxable income. The measures from LEED have no sampling error as may happen in a sample survey. This is because the database contains data belonging to all individuals with taxable income. The outputs are based on data that have been supplied by IR within 18 months after the end of the tax year.
For the latest data, see LEED .
Detailed statistics about the interaction of individuals with the labour market and their sources of income during the tax year (or year ending 31 March). These statistics are mostly person-level outputs.
Detailed statistics about the interaction of individuals with the labour market and their sources of income during the tax year (or year ending 31 March). These statistics are mostly person-level outputs.
Monthly PAYE tax data and annual tax returns with self-employment income

## Monthly employment indicator (MEI)
The MEI makes use of the more timely and detailed payday filing data available from IR starting in April 2019. All data supplied by IR within 4 weeks after the end of the month are used to produce the outputs.
For the latest data, see Employment indicators .

## Labour cost index (LCI)
The LCI measures changes in wage and salary rates for a fixed quantity and quality of labour input. It is part of the quarterly labour market release. For the latest data, see quarterly Labour market statistics .
Indicator of wage inflation (ie change in wage and salary rates that employers pay to have the same job completed to the same standard)

## Quarterly employment survey (QES)
The QES is Stats NZ’s largest subannual business survey. Its measures are part of the quarterly labour market release. For the latest data, see quarterly Labour market statistics .
Estimates of quarterly changes and levels of filled jobs, average hourly and weekly earnings, and average weekly paid hours Average ordinary-time earnings (with FTE denominator) in the December quarter is used for adjustments to National superannuation.  The annual movement in the March quarter in average total weekly earnings (with filled job denominator) is used to determine adjustments required for paid parental leave.
Estimates of quarterly changes and levels of filled jobs, average hourly and weekly earnings, and average weekly paid hours Average ordinary-time earnings (with FTE denominator) in the December quarter is used for adjustments to National superannuation.  The annual movement in the March quarter in average total weekly earnings (with filled job denominator) is used to determine adjustments required for paid parental leave.

## Business employment data (BED)
The BED uses data from the MEI with the exclusion of self-employed who pay themselves a wage or salary. It is part of the quarterly collection of business financial data that feeds into the production of the Gross domestic product.
For the latest data, see Business demography .
Detailed measures of quarterly filled jobs and earnings. The BED is associated with full-quarter measures, or measures that cover each week in the quarter.
Detailed measures of quarterly filled jobs and earnings. The BED is associated with full-quarter measures, or measures that cover each week in the quarter.

## Quarterly Linked employer-employee data (LEED)
Quarterly LEED is created by linking a longitudinal series of IR’s PAYE data to employer data from Stats NZ’s Business frame. The measures from LEED have no sampling error as may happen in a sample survey. This is because LEED contains data belonging to all individuals in the PAYE system. All PAYE data supplied by IR within 12 months after the end of the quarter are used to produce the outputs.
For the latest data, see LEED .
Detailed statistics about levels and changes in quarterly job and worker flow, and filled jobs. Quarterly job and worker flows are gross flows underlying net changes in filled jobs.
Detailed statistics about levels and changes in quarterly job and worker flow, and filled jobs. Quarterly job and worker flows are gross flows underlying net changes in filled jobs.
Monthly PAYE tax data aggregated from payday records for quarters from June 2019 onwards. Prior to the June 2019 quarter, the series is based on data from the Employer’s monthly schedule (EMS).
Monthly PAYE tax data aggregated from payday records for quarters from June 2019 onwards. Prior to the June 2019 quarter, the series is based on data from the Employer’s monthly schedule (EMS).

## Business demography statistics (BDS)
BDS provides an annual snapshot as at February of the structure and characteristics of New Zealand businesses. It includes employee counts obtained from PAYE data in LEED.
For the latest data, see Business demography .

## Weekly employment indicator (WEI)
The WEI is an experimental measure that provides an early indicator of employment and labour market changes in a more a timely manner than the monthly employment indicators series. Three time-lag series are included in the release: the 6-day lag series includes paid jobs with a pay period equal to or less than 7 days, the 20-day series covers paid jobs with pay periods of 14 days or fewer. The 34-day series includes all paid jobs regardless of their pay period.
For the latest data, see Employment indicators .
Early indicator of changes in weekly paid jobs, and median weekly wages and salaries. (A Monday to Sunday week is used for the measures.)
Early indicator of changes in weekly paid jobs, and median weekly wages and salaries. (A Monday to Sunday week is used for the measures.)

## Text alternative for Filled jobs vs employed persons
Diagram shows the intersection between labour supply and demand concepts. These intersections are represented by overlapping circles in the diagram. Businesses are shown to produce labour demand, in the form of unmet labour demand (or job vacancies) and filled jobs (measured by business employment data). Households are shown to produce labour supply, in the form of jobless people (those unemployed or not in the labour force) and employed persons (measured by household employment data). The count of employed persons covers paid employees, employers, self-employed without employees, and unpaid family workers. Filled jobs and employed persons have some overlap, in the form of paid employees filling a job in a business. The difference between the count of filled jobs and paid employees can be attributed to factors like additional jobs held by multiple job holders and jobs filled by employees not within the usual resident population. Total employed persons also include some people who aren’t included in the filled job counts, such as employers, self-employed people without employees, and unpaid family workers.
